title: Oliver Twist
author: Charles Dickens
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Oliver Twist" book by Charles Dickens?
Options:
A. The tinker jerked awake just as abruptly as he had dozed off. Brittles went along with these terms; and the party felt somewhat reassured upon opening the shutters and realizing it was now morning, so they proceeded upstairs, the dogs going first. The two women, afraid to remain below, trailed behind. Acting on Mr. Giles's advice, they all spoke boisterously to alert any wicked people outside that they had strength in numbers; and through a masterful tactical maneuver devised by the ingenious Mr. Giles, the dogs' tails were sharply pinched in the hall, causing them to bark ferociously.  
B. The tinker woke up suddenly, just as he had fallen asleep. Brittles agreed to these terms; and feeling a little more confident after opening the shutters and seeing it was light out, the group went upstairs, with the dogs in front. The two scared women came up last. Following Mr. Giles's recommendation, they all talked loudly to signal to any bad people outside that their group was large. And in a clever strategic idea thought up by Mr. Giles, the dogs' tails were pinched hard in the hall to make them bark aggressively.
C. The tinker woke up suddenly, just as he had fallen asleep before. Brittles agreed to these conditions; and feeling a bit more confident after opening the shutters and seeing it was daylight, the group went upstairs, with the dogs leading the way. The two frightened women came up last. Following Mr. Giles's suggestion, they all talked loudly to signal to any ill-intentioned people outside that their group was large. And in a clever strategic move dreamed up by Mr. Giles, the dogs' tails were pinched hard in the hall to make them bark aggressively.
D. So am I,' said the tinker, waking up, as suddenly as he had fallen asleep. Brittles capitualated on these terms; and the party being somewhat re-assured by the discovery (made on throwing open the shutters) that it was now broad day, took their way upstairs; with the dogs in front. The two women, who were afraid to stay below, brought up the rear. By the advice of Mr. Giles, they all talked very loud, to warn any evil-disposed person outside, that they were strong in numbers; and by a master-stoke of policy, originating in the brain of the same ingenious gentleman, the dogs' tails were well pinched, in the hall, to make them bark savagely.
Answer:

options.A: The tinker jerked awake just as abruptly as he had dozed off. Brittles went along with these terms; and the party felt somewhat reassured upon opening the shutters and realizing it was now morning, so they proceeded upstairs, the dogs going first. options.B: The tinker woke up suddenly, just as he had fallen asleep. Brittles agreed to these conditions; and feeling a bit more confident after opening the shutters and seeing it was daylight, the group went upstairs, with the dogs leading the way. Brittles capitualated on these terms; and the party being somewhat re-assured by the discovery (made on throwing open the shutters) that it was now broad day, took their way upstairs; with the dogs in front.
